behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is local backoff math/state progression. No module handoff, real I/O, or external timing primitive is required to expose wrong doubling/cap logic.
behaviors.test_description: Test the backoff calculator (or retry-state function) with consecutive failure counts and config max; assert sequence like 100,200,400,... then capped value.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is specifically at a module boundary: HTTP response classification feeds retry policy timing. Unit-only tests of classifier or calculator can miss handoff/contract mismatches.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry orchestrator + response parser + backoff policy using stubbed transport responses (e.g., 429 with headers/body variants). Assert it classifies as rate-limit and schedules expected delay.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Real failure appears in orchestration across retry state, timer/scheduler, and request execution. Real network is not required; in-process stubs plus controllable clock can reproduce it.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake clock + stub transport that returns rate-limit then success. Call `call()` once; advance time; assert second send uses same request payload and final resolved value equals successful response.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Typed error emerges from combined retry counting + terminal error mapping in `call()` flow. A pure unit of the error class is insufficient to catch miswired propagation.
behaviors.test_description: Stub transport to always return rate-limit. Execute `call()` through max retries; assert rejection is instance of `QuotaExhaustedError` (and not generic Error) with expected metadata.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly spans multiple internal modules (retry loop + backoff + quota tracker). No real OS/network/subprocess needed; controlled in-process dependencies can expose non-termination bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Run `call()` with always-rate-limited stub and fake clock; advance through all scheduled waits; assert promise settles after expected attempts and no extra retries are enqueued.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Failure mode is shared-state coordination across concurrent callers and quota manager module. Unit tests of isolated functions miss cross-caller state interaction.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/quota bucket, fire multiple concurrent `call()` invocations with stub transport, and assert observed permit/retry behavior reflects one shared quota (not per-caller independent quotas).

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Ordering bug appears at queue/scheduler boundary under concurrency, not within one pure function alone. Real infrastructure is unnecessary; deterministic in-process scheduling is enough.
behaviors.test_description: Queue several calls while quota blocks sends, then release capacity stepwise via stubbed quota/clock; assert transport send order matches submission order (FIFO).
